title: Colossal Magnetoresistance and Phonon Driven Exchange Dynamics in Eu$_5$Sn$_2$As$_6$
authors: Luke Pritchard Cairns, Kohtaro Yamakawa, Shengzhi Zhang, Youzhe Chen, Bernard Field, Rainer Reczek, Ryan P. Day, Joel E. Moore, Marcelo Jaime, Sinead M. Griffin, Robert J. Birgeneau, James G. Analytis
abstract: The emergence of colossal magnetoresistance in a new generation of Eu$^{2+}$-based antiferromagnets is intriguing given stark contrasts to the archetypal perovskite manganites and doped Eu-chalcogenides. In this study the thermal conductivity and magnetostriction of Eu$_5$Sn$_2$As$_6$ -- one such representative -- have been measured to better understand the role of the crystal lattice. Both properties are strongly field-dependent and mirror the magnetization, saturating once the Eu$^{2+}$ moments are polarized. The field-enhancement of the phonon-dominated thermal conductivity is interpreted through the lifting of a degeneracy of spin configurations, and the subsequent saturation due to quenched magnetostrain in high field. Comparison with spin-glass insulators suggests that this phenomenon is not a byproduct but rather the driver of electron delocalization due to the suppression of strong phonon scattering arising from exchange frustration.
summary: This paper investigates the mechanism behind colossal magnetoresistance in a new class of europium-based antiferromagnets. It reveals that the resistance change is driven by a magnetic field lifting the degeneracy of frustrated spin configurations, which reduces phonon scattering. This suggests that lattice-mediated exchange dynamics are the primary driver of electron delocalization in this material.
main_problem: Understanding the physical origin of Colossal Magnetoresistance (CMR) in the Eu2+-based antiferromagnet Eu5Sn2As6, specifically the role of the crystal lattice and phonon-driven exchange dynamics.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that CMR is driven by the suppression of phonon scattering caused by exchange frustration; applying a magnetic field lifts the degeneracy of spin/lattice configurations, quenching magnetostrain and enhancing thermal conductivity.
method: The study combines experimental measurements (thermal conductivity, magnetostriction, neutron diffraction, and resistivity) with computational techniques (DFT+U and classical spin modeling using SU(N)NY).
model_or_system: The system is Eu5Sn2As6, a Zintl-phase stoichiometric antiferromagnet with a non-collinear magnetic ground state. The magnetic interactions are modeled using an effective Heisenberg-like Hamiltonian with a small Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya term.
key_observables: Thermal conductivity, electrical resistivity, magnetostriction, magnetization, specific heat, and magnetic Bragg peaks.
important_parameters: Magnetic transitions at TN' = 9.4 K and TN = 10.3 K; CMR magnitude up to a factor of 6000; band gap of ~35-38 meV; Debye temperature of ~21 K.
main_assumptions: The thermal conductivity is assumed to be phonon-dominated; the electronic transport is modeled using the semiclassical Boltzmann transport equation assuming a constant relaxation time.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Crystal structure and magnetic neutron diffraction; Fig 2: Temperature/field dependence of thermal conductivity, specific heat, and resistivity; Fig 3: Field dependence of magnetostriction, magnetization, and thermal conductivity; Fig 4: Magnetic rocking scans; Fig 5: Electronic band structure and DOS.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CMR phenomenon in Eu5Sn2As6, presents experimental characterization of magnetic and thermal properties, details the neutron diffraction and DFT results to establish the magnetic/electronic structure, and concludes with a proposed mechanism involving exchange-striction-driven phonon scattering suppression.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Crystal structure of Eu5Sn2As6. 1, 2 and 3 la- bel the three inequivalent Eu sites. J22 and J13 label the two nearest neighbour bonds, with separation between the Eu sites of 3.67876 Å and 3.69799 Å respectively. The ground state magnetic structure determined by neutron scattering is shown by the red arrows. (b) Contour plots showing the spe- cific heat and field-derivative of the magnetisation for fields oriented ˆH ∥z (∥c [19]). (c) Neutron diffraction results for Eu5Sn2As6, MCU is monitor counts collected for one second exposure of the neutron beam. The transitions at TN and TN′ are indicated by the vertical dashed lines.

FIG. 2. (a) Thermal conductivity of Eu5Sn2As6 as a func- tion of temperature for a range of fields applied ∥z (left axis, closed circles), and the specific heat at 0 and 9 T (right axis, open triangles), with the estimated phonon contribution sub- tracted (see SM). (b) Electrical resistivity under the same conditions. In both cases the current is also applied ∥z.

FIG. 3. (a) Magnetostriction, (b) magnetisation, and (c) ther- mal conductivity of Eu5Sn2As6 as a function of applied field ˆH ∥z, at a range of temperatures. κ0 and L0 refer to the values at zero-field. The magnetostriction data has been off- set for clarity. The thermal gradient is also applied ∥z. In (b), the dashed line is at 7µB/Eu, the expected saturation moment of a free Eu2+ ion.
FIG. 5. Electronic band structure and density of states, in- cluding the contribution from each orbital. Calculated at U = 10 eV. Plotted with sumo [49].
